synthesis:
### QE ArXiv Watch Weekly — Week ending Mar 30, 2026 (5 papers)

If you’ve ever wanted to *optimize a design over a continuous knob-space* (sensor settings, control profiles, sampling schedules) without paying the “re-run inference from scratch” tax every time you twist the knob—this week had a couple of genuinely practical ideas. The common thread: make the objective differentiable/cheap enough that design becomes something you can *search*, not something you do by hand-waving and a few grid points.

#### Design when your likelihood is a simulator: amortize the Bayes factor
Leeney et al. push a neat trick for discovery-style experiments: instead of estimating evidence/Bayes factors separately for each proposed design, they train a neural ratio estimator that *takes the design as an input* (Conditional Neural Bayes Ratio Estimation, cNBRE). After that, you can sweep a continuous design space quickly and optimize a decision utility like “probability the Bayes factor clears a 3σ threshold.”

What’s compelling for practitioners isn’t the cosmology application—it’s the workflow: one amortized surrogate that supports global exploration, and potentially gradient-based optimization because the surrogate is differentiable. Their validation against nested sampling (1,600 matched simulations) is reassuring in the decision-relevant regime; the surrogate saturates only at very high log Bayes factors where, operationally, you already “won.”

Imagine doing this for “detect a weak degradation signal” with designs like sampling rate, test duration, sensor placement, filtering choices—anything continuous.

#### OED for controlled dynamical systems: EIG without the pain (and adjoints that actually work)
Montella et al. take on Bayesian optimal experimental design in ODE-driven systems where you choose both controls and when/where to measure under a budget. Exact expected information gain (EIG) is often too expensive, and a lot of approximations don’t play nicely with adjoint methods (so optimization becomes brittle).

Their move is to build *time-additive surrogate objectives* that are adjoint-compatible. Two flavors matter:

- An “instantaneous/myopic” surrogate that’s easy but provably **upper-bounds** EIG (it double-counts information because it ignores redundancy between time points). Useful—if you remember it can over-focus measurements.
- A “Gaussian-tilting” surrogate that reweights the prior using accumulated Fisher information; it’s **exact in linear–Gaussian** cases and empirically more robust under non-Gaussian or multimodal priors (with a multi-center extension).

For anyone doing system ID, accelerated life testing with controlled inputs, or adaptive diagnostics: this is the most “ready to try” design math this week—especially if you already have adjoints in your simulation stack.

#### Cost-aware causal design: measure less, estimate better (front-door/back-door)
Mareis et al. bring experimental design thinking to a very QE problem: measurement is expensive, and you can’t always observe everything on everyone. They consider staged data collection (treatment for all, mediators for some, outcomes for some) with user-defined costs, and derive *optimal sampling propensities* that minimize asymptotic variance under a budget—grounded in semiparametric efficiency / influence functions.

The headline is not theoretical elegance (though it’s there); it’s the practical result: **~5% to ~32% variance reduction at matched cost** versus “measure everything” in several datasets, including an industrial assembly example (back-door case). The optimal policy often hits 0/1 boundaries—exactly what you’d expect when some units are high-leverage and worth the extra measurement.

If you’re designing observational studies, audits, or instrumentation plans with partial measurement, this is a strong template for “spend the budget where it moves the estimator.”

#### Reliability: calibrated neural operators for time-dependent failure probability
Garg et al. target time-dependent reliability for nonlinear dynamical systems under stochastic excitation—where brute-force Monte Carlo is punishing. They learn an operator from input load histories to full response trajectories (DeepONet-like), add Bayesian predictive uncertainty, then *conformalize* intervals to get finite-sample coverage guarantees.

Two things stand out for reliability folks:
1) conformal calibration actually fixes the classic under-coverage problem (they report ≥95% coverage across time steps, while the uncalibrated model misses often), and  
2) the “spiking” branch network is aimed at energy-efficient inference—interesting if you care about deployment at scale.

If you’re using surrogates to compute first-passage failure probabilities, the conformal layer is the most transferable idea: it’s a bolt-on sanity check for uncertainty.

#### A quick detour: LLMs as DDD sparring partners (not architects)
Eisenreich et al. report an industrial case study using LLM prompting to assist Domain-Driven Design. The punchline is pragmatic: LLMs help a lot with early artifacts (glossaries, event flows, candidate bounded contexts), but errors compound and the downstream “aggregate design” and “map to architecture” steps become unreliable without heavy human steering.

For QE teams building internal platforms/tools: treat LLMs like a fast facilitator for eliciting assumptions—not a design authority.

---

**Question to take into next week:** We’re getting better at *designing* experiments under simulation, control, and cost constraints—but are we ready to standardize “calibration checks” (like conformal coverage) for design surrogates too, not just predictive models? That feels like the missing bridge between clever optimization and trustworthy decisions.

papers.title: Conditional Neural Bayes Ratio Estimation for Experimental Design Optimisation
papers.authors: S. A. K. Leeney|T. Gessey-Jones|W. J. Handley|E. de Lera Acedo|H. T. J. Bevins|J. L. Tutt

papers.title: Adjoint-Compatible Surrogates of the Expected Information Gain for Optimal Experimental Design in Controlled Dynamical Systems
Automating Domain-Driven Design: Experience with a Prompting Framework
CoNBONet: Conformalized Neuroscience-inspired Bayesian Operator Network for Reliability Analysis
